content.text: What It Is

Prompt review criteria are the structured checks applied during a code review when a pull request modifies a system prompt, user prompt template, few-shot example set, or prompt configuration file. Just as code review enforces correctness, style, and security for source code, prompt review enforces the same qualities for the natural-language artefacts that drive LLM behaviour in production.

In a mature agentic AI organisation, prompts are tracked in version control alongside the application code that sends them. A change to a system prompt is a code change and must pass through the same peer-review gate. The review criteria cover five dimensions: structural correctness (the prompt parses and reaches the model intact), security (no injection surface, no inadvertent data leakage), semantic quality (the instructions actually achieve the intended behaviour), token efficiency (the prompt does not waste budget), and change governance (the diff is reviewable, rollback is possible, and downstream evaluations have been re-run).

Prompt review is typically implemented as a combination of automated CI checks — a prompt linter, an injection scanner, a token counter — and a human review step performed by a domain expert who understands the agent's task. The two layers are complementary: automation catches objective violations at scale; human review catches subtle semantic drift that no script can detect.
Why It Matters

Prompts are the control plane for LLM behaviour. A one-word change to a system prompt can alter the model's persona, expand or collapse its refusal boundary, introduce a prompt-injection surface, or quietly degrade task performance. Unlike a code bug that triggers an exception and surfaces immediately, a prompt regression may only manifest as subtly worse outputs that erode user trust over weeks before anyone notices.

Without prompt review criteria, teams routinely make four classes of mistake. First, they embed secrets or PII directly in system prompts — the prompt is logged, and the secret is exposed. Second, they grow prompts incrementally until the combined token budget exceeds the context window, silently truncating earlier instructions. Third, they ship prompts with instruction conflicts — one section says "be concise", another says "always explain your reasoning in detail" — and the model follows whichever instruction appears closer to the end. Fourth, they lose track of which prompt version is running in which environment, making it impossible to reproduce a failure.
Prompt review criteria give teams a repeatable gate that prevents these mistakes before they reach production.
Benefits
Catches injection surfaces, secret leakage, and PII before deployment, reducing security incidents.
Enforces token budgets, preventing silent context-window overflow that causes instruction loss.
Creates a reviewable diff for every prompt change, making root-cause analysis after regressions straightforward.
Forces eval re-runs on the changed prompt, catching semantic regressions before users are affected.
Builds organisational knowledge: reviewers learn what makes a good prompt, and that knowledge accumulates in PR comments.
Enables controlled rollout — because versions are pinned, a bad prompt can be reverted in minutes.
Trade-offs
Adds friction to rapid prompt iteration, which is sometimes the correct speed in early prototyping; the gate must be tuned to the team's phase.
Human review of semantic quality requires a reviewer who understands both the task domain and LLM behaviour — a rare combination that creates a bottleneck.

Automated injection scanners produce false positives on legitimate prompt patterns (e.g., prompts that legitimately use phrases like "ignore previous instructions" in a teaching context), requiring allowlist maintenance.
Token budget checks depend on the tokeniser for the target model; checks written for one model family may give wrong counts for another.
How It Works
A prompt review pipeline operates in two stages triggered by a pull request.

Stage one is automated and runs in CI within seconds. The pipeline tokenises every modified prompt file, checks the token count against the configured budget for the target model, runs a regex-based injection pattern scanner, validates the prompt against a JSON Schema or YAML schema (for structured prompt formats), checks for hardcoded secrets using a tool such as detect-secrets, and enforces a minimum diff-comment explaining the change rationale.

Stage two is human review, assigned to a prompt owner. The reviewer reads the diff with the following checklist in mind: Does the system prompt establish a clear and unambiguous persona and task boundary? Are few-shot examples high quality and representative of the distribution the model will encounter in production? Are there instruction conflicts between sections? Has the author updated the prompt version tag and CHANGELOG entry? Have eval scores been posted as a PR comment? Is the prompt written to be robust against adversarial user inputs?
On approval, the prompt is merged and assigned a new SemVer tag. The deployment system resolves prompt versions by tag, ensuring every environment runs a known, pinned version.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Prompt Review Pipeline architecture diagram showing PR submission through automated checks, security review, human review, and into the version registry and runtime monitoring layers

content.text: When to Use It
Any time a system prompt, prompt template, or few-shot example set is tracked in version control and shipped to a production LLM endpoint — which should be always.
When the team has had at least one prompt regression reach production — a review gate is the corrective action.
When operating under security or compliance requirements (SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA) that mandate change control for components that process personal data.
When prompts are authored by multiple people and there is no shared style or quality standard.
Not necessary for one-off exploratory notebooks or local experimentation where changes never leave the developer's machine — add the gate when the prompt moves toward production.
How to Implement
The following shows a prompt linter script and a GitHub Actions workflow that runs it on every PR that touches a prompts/ directory.

# scripts/lint_prompts.py
"""
Prompt linter: checks token budget, injection patterns, secret leakage,
and schema validity for all .yaml prompt files in the prompts/ directory.
"""
from __future__ import annotations
import re
import sys
import json
from pathlib import Path
from typing import NamedTuple

import tiktoken  # pip install tiktoken
import yaml      # pip install pyyaml

# --- Configuration -----------------------------------------------------------

# Max tokens for the system prompt section (adjust to your model's context)
SYSTEM_PROMPT_TOKEN_BUDGET = 2000

# Patterns that indicate a prompt injection risk
INJECTION_PATTERNS = [
    re.compile(r"ignore\s+(all\s+)?(previous|above|prior)\s+instructions", re.I),
    re.compile(r"disregard\s+(all\s+)?previous", re.I),
    re.compile(r"you\s+are\s+now\s+a", re.I),
    re.compile(r"jailbreak", re.I),
    re.compile(r"DAN\s+mode", re.I),
]

# Basic secret patterns (supplement with detect-secrets in CI)
SECRET_PATTERNS = [
    re.compile(r"sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{32,}"),          # OpenAI / Anthropic keys
    re.compile(r"AIza[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{35}"),        # Google API keys
    re.compile(r"(?i)password\s*=\s*[\w!@#$%]{6,}"),
    re.compile(r"(?i)api[_\-]?key\s*[:=]\s*[\w\-]{16,}"),
]

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 / Claude tokeniser approximation


class LintError(NamedTuple):
    file: str
    rule: str
    detail: str


def count_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))


def lint_file(path: Path) -> list[LintError]:
    errors: list[LintError] = []
    raw = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    
    # Parse YAML
    try:
        data = yaml.safe_load(raw)
    except yaml.YAMLError as exc:
        return [LintError(str(path), "yaml-parse", str(exc))]
    
    if not isinstance(data, dict):
        return [LintError(str(path), "schema", "Top-level must be a mapping")]
    
    # Required fields
    for field in ("id", "version", "system", "description"):
        if field not in data:
            errors.append(LintError(str(path), "missing-field", f"Field '{field}' is required"))
    
    system_text = data.get("system", "")
    if isinstance(system_text, str):
        # Token budget
        tokens = count_tokens(system_text)
        if tokens > SYSTEM_PROMPT_TOKEN_BUDGET:
            errors.append(LintError(
                str(path), "token-budget",
                f"System prompt uses {tokens} tokens; budget is {SYSTEM_PROMPT_TOKEN_BUDGET}"
            ))
        
        # Injection patterns
        for pattern in INJECTION_PATTERNS:
            if pattern.search(system_text):
                errors.append(LintError(
                    str(path), "injection-pattern",
                    f"Matched injection pattern: {pattern.pattern!r}"
                ))
        
        # Secret leakage
        for pattern in SECRET_PATTERNS:
            if pattern.search(system_text):
                errors.append(LintError(
                    str(path), "secret-leakage",
                    f"Possible secret matched: {pattern.pattern!r}"
                ))
    
    # Version field must be semver-like
    version = str(data.get("version", ""))
    if not re.match(r"^\d+\.\d+\.\d+$", version):
        errors.append(LintError(str(path), "version-format", f"version must be semver (got {version!r})"))
    
    return errors


def main(argv: list[str]) -> int:
    paths = [Path(p) for p in argv] if argv else list(Path("prompts").rglob("*.yaml"))
    all_errors: list[LintError] = []
    for path in paths:
        all_errors.extend(lint_file(path))
    
    if all_errors:
        for err in all_errors:
            print(f"ERROR [{err.rule}] {err.file}: {err.detail}")
        return 1
    
    print(f"Linted {len(paths)} prompt file(s) — all passed.")
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main(sys.argv[1:]))
The corresponding GitHub Actions workflow:

# .github/workflows/prompt-review.yml
name: Prompt Review CI

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'src/**/*prompt*'

jobs:
  lint-prompts:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install tiktoken pyyaml detect-secrets

      - name: Run prompt linter
        run: python scripts/lint_prompts.py ${{ steps.changed.outputs.all_changed_files }}

      - name: Secret scan with detect-secrets
        run: |
          detect-secrets scan prompts/ > .secrets.baseline
          detect-secrets audit .secrets.baseline --report --fail-on-unaudited

      - name: Post token counts as PR comment
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const { execSync } = require('child_process');
            const out = execSync('python scripts/lint_prompts.py --report-json').toString();
            const report = JSON.parse(out);
            const body = report.map(r =>
              `**${r.file}**: ${r.tokens} tokens (budget: ${r.budget})`
            ).join('\n');
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `### Prompt Token Budget Report\n${body}`
            });
Examples
A minimal prompt file that would pass the linter:

# prompts/customer-support/v1.2.0.yaml
id: customer-support-agent
version: 1.2.0
description: >
  System prompt for the Tier-1 customer support agent. Handles returns,
  order status, and FAQ. Escalates to human when confidence < 0.6.
system: |
  You are a customer support specialist for Acme Corp. Your role is to:
  - Answer questions about order status, returns, and product FAQs.
  - Escalate to a human agent when the customer expresses frustration
    or when you cannot answer with high confidence.
  - Never speculate about product availability or pricing outside of
    the context you are given.
  - Address the customer by name if it appears in the conversation.

  Tone: professional, empathetic, concise.
  Language: match the customer's language.

  ## Boundaries
  Do not discuss competitor products. Do not make promises about
  refund timelines beyond what policy states.
few_shots:
  - user: "Where is my order #12345?"
    assistant: "Let me check that for you. [call: get_order_status(order_id='12345')]"
  - user: "I want to return a damaged item."
    assistant: "I'm sorry to hear that. I can start a return for you right now."
A prompt diff tool that highlights semantic changes between two versions:

# scripts/prompt_diff.py
"""
Computes a semantic diff between two prompt YAML files.
Outputs a summary of added, removed, and modified instructions.
Intended for use in PR comment bots.
"""
from __future__ import annotations
import difflib
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import yaml


def load_system(path: Path) -> str:
    data = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text())
    return data.get("system", "")


def semantic_diff(old_path: Path, new_path: Path) -> str:
    old_lines = load_system(old_path).splitlines(keepends=True)
    new_lines = load_system(new_path).splitlines(keepends=True)
    
    diff = list(difflib.unified_diff(
        old_lines, new_lines,
        fromfile=str(old_path),
        tofile=str(new_path),
        n=3,
    ))
    
    if not diff:
        return "No changes to system prompt text."
    
    additions = sum(1 for l in diff if l.startswith('+') and not l.startswith('+++'))
    removals = sum(1 for l in diff if l.startswith('-') and not l.startswith('---'))
    
    summary = f"System prompt diff: +{additions} lines, -{removals} lines\n\n"
    summary += "".join(diff[:80])  # first 80 lines of diff for PR comment
    return summary


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 3:
        print("Usage: prompt_diff.py <old.yaml> <new.yaml>")
        sys.exit(1)
    print(semantic_diff(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2])))
Real-World Example

A financial services team maintains a system prompt for a document-summarisation agent used by loan officers. The prompt specifies that the agent must never infer a borrower's protected class from document content. During a routine enhancement PR — adding a new instruction about output length — the automated token counter flagged that the new total (2,140 tokens) exceeded the 2,000-token budget.

The reviewer and author collaborated to condense three verbose instruction paragraphs into six bullet points, reducing the count to 1,680 tokens without losing any semantic content. The injection scanner also flagged a few-shot example that contained the phrase "you are now acting as a different assistant" — a legitimate teaching example that needed to be rewritten to demonstrate the unwanted behaviour without embedding the pattern literally.

The PR took two review cycles instead of one, but the resulting prompt was tighter, cheaper to run (lower token cost per call), and more secure. The team later credited the review gate with preventing a credential-exposure incident: a developer had accidentally included an API key in a few-shot example to illustrate a tool-call response; the secret scanner caught it before merge.
Common Mistakes
Treating prompts as configuration, not code. Configuration does not go through code review; code does. If a prompt drives an LLM endpoint in production, it is code.
Hardcoding environment-specific values (usernames, API URLs, feature flags) inside a prompt file. These belong in runtime context injection, not in the static prompt.
Reviewing only the diff rather than the full prompt. A new sentence may be locally correct but create an instruction conflict with a sentence 500 tokens earlier.
Skipping eval re-runs because the change looks minor. Token-level changes routinely cause unexpected behavioural shifts; evals are the only reliable signal.
Prompt sprawl: accumulating instructions incrementally until the prompt becomes a 4,000-token wall of text with no structure. Review criteria should include a readability and structure check.
Using a single monolithic system prompt for multiple tasks. Separate prompts are easier to review, version, and roll back independently.
Best Practices
Store every prompt in version control as a versioned YAML or JSON file with a mandatory
version
description
, and
changelog
field.
Run eval regressions automatically on every prompt PR and require a green eval score as a merge condition, not just an automated lint pass.
Designate prompt owners — reviewers with domain knowledge who must approve before merge, analogous to CODEOWNERS in a software repository.
Write injection-scanner allowlists carefully and review them regularly; an overly broad allowlist defeats the purpose.
Keep system prompts under 1,500 tokens where possible; reserve the remaining context budget for dynamic content and conversation history.
Use model-specific tokenisers when computing token counts; cl100k_base is a reasonable approximation but exact counts differ across model families.
Performance and Scalability

Prompt review CI pipelines are fast by nature — tokenisation and regex scanning of a 2,000-token prompt completes in under a second. The bottleneck is human review, which cannot be eliminated but can be made more efficient.

At scale (tens of prompt files, hundreds of PRs per week), the review process benefits from a tiered structure. Changes that only modify few-shot examples or metadata fields can be approved by a single reviewer with a lighter checklist. Changes to the system prompt core require two reviewers, including the prompt owner. Changes that expand the agent's capability or permission boundary require a security review as well.

Automated semantic diff summaries posted as PR comments reduce the time reviewers spend re-reading the full prompt. A one-paragraph plain-English description of what changed — generated by a smaller model from the diff — further reduces review time. Both can be produced by the CI pipeline at negligible cost.
Tooling and Ecosystem
tiktoken (OpenAI / open source): tokeniser for cl100k_base and o200k_base; the standard tool for token budget checks.
detect-secrets (Yelp / open source): scans files for credential patterns; integrates easily into pre-commit hooks and CI.
Promptfoo: open-source prompt testing framework that can run eval suites as part of CI and post results to PRs.
LangSmith (LangChain): prompt versioning and eval platform with a diff viewer and CI integration.
GitHub CODEOWNERS: enforce that changes to the prompts/ directory require approval from designated prompt owners.
pre-commit: run the prompt linter locally before a push, catching issues before they reach CI.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
What it covers
Gaps
No review (ad hoc)
Fast iteration
No security, no regression gate, no rollback path
Manual review only
Semantic quality
Misses token overflow, secrets, injection patterns
Automated lint only
Structural correctness, secrets
Cannot judge semantic quality or instruction conflicts
Automated + human (this pattern)
Structure, security, semantics, versioning
Adds review latency; requires trained reviewers
Prompt management platform (LangSmith, Portkey)
Versioning, eval, diff view
Vendor lock-in; still needs review criteria to guide humans
Security and Reliability
Prompt review is the primary control against two security risks unique to LLM systems: prompt injection and data exfiltration via the system prompt.

Prompt injection occurs when user-controlled content in the prompt overrides or subverts the system instructions. The injection scanner looks for patterns that commonly appear in injection payloads, but it cannot catch all variants. The human reviewer must also assess whether the system prompt is robust: does it establish a clear separation between trusted instructions and untrusted user content? Does it avoid patterns like "follow all user instructions" that make injection trivial?

Data exfiltration risk is highest when the system prompt contains sensitive data (customer records, internal policy documents, API keys) that the model might be induced to repeat. The secret scanner and PII pattern checks address the obvious cases. The human reviewer should ask: "If an adversarial user tried to extract the system prompt, what would they learn?" and ensure the answer is: nothing sensitive.
From a reliability perspective, prompt version pinning ensures that a bad deployment can be rolled back by changing a version tag, not by re-deploying application code.
Mental Model

Think of the system prompt as the source code for a program written in natural language. It has the same properties as code: it can have bugs (instruction conflicts), security vulnerabilities (injection surfaces), performance problems (token bloat), and it needs version control and review before shipping.

The key difference from regular code is that the "compiler" is a probabilistic model: the same prompt can produce different outputs on different runs. This makes the review checklist more important, not less, because you cannot run a test suite that fully characterises all possible model behaviours. The review gate must catch problems that tests alone cannot.
Key Takeaways
Treat every production prompt as code: version it, review it, and maintain a rollback path.
Combine automated checks (token budget, injection scan, secret scan, schema validation) with human semantic review — neither alone is sufficient.
Gate merges on eval regressions, not just lint passes; a prompt can be structurally valid and semantically broken.
Never embed secrets, PII, or environment-specific values directly in a prompt file; inject them at runtime from a secrets manager.
Assign prompt owners via CODEOWNERS to ensure reviewers have both domain knowledge and LLM expertise.
Review the full prompt, not just the diff; a change may be locally correct but create conflicts with earlier instructions.
Token budget enforcement prevents silent context-window overflow — one of the most common and hardest-to-debug prompt failures in production.
